title: Variables & Type Inference
description: Learn how Crystal infers types from assignments, how reassignment narrows union types, and why explicit annotations are optional in most code.

concept.definition: Crystal infers variable types at compile time from assigned values. You can reassign variables, and Crystal tracks possible types as unions when needed.
concept.analogy: Think of Crystal as a strict librarian who reads your notes and deduces category labels automatically.
keyPoints: Type inference is static (compile-time), not dynamic
Union types appear when a variable may hold multiple types
Optional annotations improve readability in APIs

title: Data Types
description: Explore Crystal primitives and core types: Int32/Int64, Float64, Bool, Char, String, Nil, and unions.

concept.definition: Crystal has rich static types with ergonomic literals. Numeric literal suffixes and constructors help you choose exact widths.
concept.analogy: Data types are precision tools; choosing the right one avoids surprises and improves performance.
keyPoints: Integers default to Int32 in many contexts
Char uses single quotes; String uses double quotes
Nil participates in unions like String?

defaultCode:
age = 30
price = 19.99
active = true
grade = 'A'
label = "StepViz"
puts "#{age}, #{price}, #{active}, #{grade}, #{label}"

title: Functions & Return Types
description: Define methods with def, pass typed parameters, and understand implicit return of the last expression.

concept.definition: Crystal methods are defined with def. The final expression is returned implicitly, and type restrictions can be added to parameters and return values.
concept.analogy: A method is a mini machine: inputs go in, final expression comes out.
keyPoints: Last expression is the return value
Parameter type restrictions are optional but useful
Methods can be overloaded by restrictions

defaultCode:
def add(a : Int32, b : Int32) : Int32
  a + b
end

puts add(3, 4)

title: Control Flow
description: Use if/unless and case/when to branch program behavior with clean, expression-oriented style.

concept.definition: Crystal supports if/unless for conditionals and case/when for pattern-like branching. if is expression-friendly and keeps logic concise.
concept.analogy: Control flow is your program's traffic system: signals decide which road execution follows.

defaultCode:
score = 82
grade = if score >= 90
  "A"
elsif score >= 80
  "B"
else
  "C"
end
puts grade

title: Loops & Iteration
description: Iterate with times, each, while, and ranges to process sequences idiomatically.

concept.definition: Crystal favors iterator methods like each and times, but also supports while and until for explicit loop control.
concept.analogy: Iterators are conveyor belts; while loops are manual steering.
keyPoints: while loops until a condition flips

defaultCode:
sum = 0
(1..5).each do |n|
  sum += n
end
puts sum

title: Classes & Objects
description: Create classes with initialize, instance variables, and methods to model real entities.

concept.definition: Classes encapsulate state and behavior. initialize constructs instances, and @instance_vars store per-object data.
concept.analogy: A class is a blueprint; objects are built copies with their own state.

defaultCode:
class User
  def initialize(@name : String, @score : Int32)
  end

  def label
    "#{@name}: #{@score}"
  end
end

u = User.new("Ari", 10)
puts u.label

title: Modules & Mixins
description: Use modules to group constants/methods and mix shared behavior into classes.

concept.definition: Modules provide namespaces and reusable behavior. include injects instance methods; extend adds class-level methods.
concept.analogy: A module is a toolbox you can mount onto many classes.

defaultCode:
module Greeter
  def greet
    "Hello!"
  end
end

class Bot
  include Greeter
end

puts Bot.new.greet

title: Blocks & Yield
description: Understand Crystal blocks, block parameters, and yield for custom iteration helpers.

concept.definition: Blocks are anonymous chunks passed to methods. Methods can call yield to execute the block with values.
concept.analogy: A block is like a callback ticket handed to a method to run later.
keyPoints: Blocks are central to Crystal style

defaultCode:
def with_value
  yield 42
end

with_value do |n|
  puts n * 2
end

title: Collections
description: Work with Array, Hash, Tuple, and common enumerable transforms like map/select/reduce.

concept.definition: Crystal collections are strongly typed and leverage Enumerable methods for transformation and querying.
concept.analogy: Collections are data shelves; Enumerable tools help filter, rearrange, and summarize items.
keyPoints: Array and Hash are most common
Types are tracked at compile time
Tuple has fixed length and types

title: Error Handling
description: Handle failures with begin/rescue/ensure and raise custom exceptions when needed.

concept.definition: Crystal uses exceptions for error handling. begin/rescue captures runtime failures; ensure always runs cleanup logic.
concept.analogy: Think of rescue as a safety net and ensure as your final checklist.

defaultCode:
begin
  n = "12x".to_i
  puts n
rescue ex
  puts "parse failed: #{ex.message}"
ensure
  puts "done"
end

title: Macros Basics
description: Discover Crystal macros for compile-time code generation and boilerplate reduction.

concept.definition: Macros run at compile time, manipulating AST nodes to generate Crystal code before runtime.
concept.analogy: Macros are code factories that build repetitive code for you during compilation.
keyPoints: Should be used carefully for readability

title: Fibers & Channels
description: Use lightweight fibers and channels to write concurrent Crystal programs with message passing.

concept.definition: Crystal concurrency is built on fibers and channels. spawn runs work concurrently, and Channel coordinates communication safely.
concept.analogy: Fibers are lightweight workers; channels are inboxes they use to exchange messages.

defaultCode:
ch = Channel(String).new
spawn do
  ch.send("done")
end
puts ch.receive
